messages.content:
can you improve  the scientific writting  of: 
Regardless of the environment, in the muscle of summer animals a depletion in unsaturated fatty acids, particularly in HUFA, was observed. The LC-PUFAs include the essential fatty acids (EFA) evolved in vitellogenin (yolk protein which is very rich in phospholipids) synthesis during the early phases of oocyte development and in the oocyte maturation. Hence, unsaturated fatty acids may have been mobilized from muscle to be allocated to the development of the ovary during the process of vitellogenesis , which is explained by the simultaneous raise of these FA classes in the gonads.

In muscles of summer animals, a reduction in unsaturated fatty acids, especially HUFA, was detected, independent of the environment. LC-PUFAs are comprised of essential fatty acids that play a vital role in vitellogenin synthesis, primarily in the initial phases of oocyte development and maturation. Therefore, unsaturated fatty acids could have been transferred from muscle tissue to aid ovary development during the vitellogenesis process. This is supported by the concurrent increase observed in FA classes within the gonads.

The FA composition of the muscle from the brackish and freshwater contingent showed a clear segregation, supported by the trophic markers analysis, that reflect the dietary influence promoted by the distinct food and energy sources exploited in each environment. In brackish water, Chelon ramada muscle had higher expression of n-3 and of unsaturated and longer chain FA, as C18FA and C20 FA, that suggest a greater influence of seaweeds and a more varied diet in terms of macroalgae, detritus, (Carassou 2016) and phytoplankton, especially dinoflagellates.

Distinct differences in FA composition were observed between muscles from the brackish and freshwater groups, confirming the effect of the respective dietary sources on the respective trophic markers. Specifically, in the Chelon ramada muscle within brackish water, there was a greater prevalence of n-3, unsaturated and longer chain FA such as C18FA and C20 FA. These findings imply a more diverse diet, possibly influenced by seaweeds and an assortment of macroalgae, detritus and phytoplankton, notably dinoflagellates.

The FA composition of the muscle from the brackish and freshwater contingent showed a clear segregation, supported by the trophic markers analysis, that reflect the dietary influence promoted by the distinct food and energy sources exploited in each environment. In brackish water, Chelon ramada muscle had higher expression of n-3 and of unsaturated and longer chain FA, as C18FA and C20 FA, that suggest a greater influence of seaweeds and a more varied diet in terms of macroalgae, detritus, (Carassou 2016) and phytoplankton, especially dinoflagellates.

The analysis of trophic markers supported the clear separation observed in the FA composition of muscles from the brackish and freshwater groups, which can be attributed to the distinct food and energy sources exploited in each environment. In particular, Chelon ramada muscle in brackish water exhibited a higher occurrence of n-3, unsaturated, and longer chain FAs, such as C18FA and C20 FA. These findings suggest that the diet of these organisms is more diversified and influenced by seaweeds, macroalgae, detritus, and phytoplankton, particularly dinoflagellates. (Carassou, 2016).

improve scientific writting: The FA composition of the muscle from the brackish and freshwater contingent showed a clear segregation, supported by the trophic markers analysis, that reflect the dietary influence promoted by the distinct food and energy sources exploited in each environment. In brackish water, Chelon ramada muscle had higher expression of n-3 and of unsaturated and longer chain FA, as C18FA and C20 FA, that suggest a greater influence of seaweeds and a more varied diet in terms of macroalgae, detritus, (Carassou 2016) and phytoplankton, especially dinoflagellates.

The FA composition of the muscles from the brackish and freshwater groups was found to be distinctly different, which was supported by the analysis of trophic markers. These results reflect the dietary influence of the different food and energy sources present in each environment. Specifically, in brackish water, the muscles of Chelon ramada exhibited a higher occurrence of n-3, unsaturated, and longer chain FA, such as C18FA and C20 FA. These findings suggest that the diet of Chelon ramada in brackish water is more diverse and heavily influenced by seaweeds, macroalgae, detritus (Carassou, 2016), and phytoplankton, particularly dinoflagellates.
I'm sorry, I cannot improve the scientific writing if there is no text provided. Can you please provide the text that you want me to improve the writing of?

The FA composition of the muscles from the brackish and freshwater groups was found to be distinctly different, which was supported by the analysis of trophic markers. These results reflect the dietary influence of the different food and energy sources present in each environment. Specifically, in brackish water, the muscles of Chelon ramada exhibited a higher occurrence of n-3, unsaturated, and longer chain FA, such as C18FA and C20 FA. These findings suggest that the diet of Chelon ramada in brackish water is more diverse and heavily influenced by seaweeds, macroalgae, detritus (Carassou, 2016), and phytoplankton, particularly dinoflagellates.

The FA composition of muscles was noticeably distinct between brackish and freshwater groups, with the trophic markers analysis supporting this observation. These differences reflect the varying food sources and energy availability present in each respective habitat. Specifically, the muscles of Chelon ramada residing in brackish water showed a higher occurrence of n-3, unsaturated, and longer chain FA, including C18FA and C20 FA. These results suggest that the diet of Chelon ramada in brackish water is more diverse and significantly influenced by the intake of seaweeds, macroalgae, detritus (Carassou, 2016), and phytoplankton, most notably dinoflagellates.
Moreover, they also showed higher expression of n-9 family, more precisely in C18:1w9 FA, a very important FA in B-oxidation processes for energy production

Additionally, in the muscle of Chelon ramada residing in brackish water, there was also an increase in the abundance of n-9 fatty acids, particularly in the C18:1w9 FA. This particular FA is crucial in the B-oxidation process, as it plays an essential role in energy production.

improve scientific writting: On the other hand, freshwater animals showed a very clear predominance of the n-7 family, associated with a high expression of the C16:1W7 FA, an important marker of freshwater epiphytic microalgae (Carassou et al.,2017) namely diatoms, and the principal FA of the enzyme ∆9 desaturase of all MUFA, both in plants and animals. Both MUFA may also be used as important sources of energy during larva development (Ortega and Mourente, 2010). The low expression of long chain FA in freshwater show that the sources of some FAs remained partially or totally planktonic (Koussoroplis et al., 2010) and the expression of microbial FA, as C15:0 (Bec et al. 2003, Dalsgaard et al. 2003, Jaschinski et al. 2008), even in lower expression, also suggest that the diet of both contingents is distinct in terms of FA availability.

On the flip side, freshwater animals displayed a noticeable prevalence of n-7 FAs, which was strongly associated with the high occurrence of C16:1W7 FA. This FA is known to be a crucial marker of freshwater epiphytic microalgae (Carassou et al., 2017), chiefly diatoms, and the primary FA utilized by Δ9 desaturase enzymes across all MUFA (both in plants and animals). Both MUFAs may also serve as essential sources of energy for larval development (Ortega and Mourente, 2010). The limited occurrence of long-chain FAs suggests that the sources of some FAs were probably partially or completely planktonic (Koussoroplis et al., 2010), while the presence of microbial FA, including C15:0 (Bec et al., 2003; Dalsgaard et al., 2003; Jaschinski et al., 2008), albeit in reduced amounts, implies that the diet in both groups differed in FA composition and availability.

On the other hand, freshwater animals showed a very clear predominance of the n-7 family, associated with a high expression of the C16:1W7 FA, an important marker of freshwater epiphytic microalgae (Carassou et al.,2017) namely diatoms, and the principal FA of the enzyme ∆9 desaturase of all MUFA, both in plants and animals. Both MUFA may also be used as important sources of energy during larva development (Ortega and Mourente, 2010). The low expression of long chain FA in freshwater show that the sources of some FAs remained partially or totally planktonic (Koussoroplis et al., 2010) and the expression of microbial FA, as C15:0 (Bec et al. 2003, Dalsgaard et al. 2003, Jaschinski et al. 2008), even in lower expression, also suggest that the diet of both contingents is distinct in terms of FA availability.

In contrast, the freshwater animals demonstrated a significant abundance of n-7 FAs, which was attributed to the high levels of C16:1W7 FA detected. This particular FA has been identified as a critical marker of freshwater epiphytic microalgae (Carassou et al., 2017), in particular diatoms, and is a significant component of Δ9 desaturase enzymes responsible for MUFA synthesis in both plants and animals. MUFA, including C16:1W7, may serve as vital energy sources during larval development (Ortega and Mourente, 2010). The scarcity of long-chain FAs observed in freshwater animals indicates that the sources of some FAs were most likely partly or entirely planktonic (Koussoroplis et al., 2010). The presence of microbial FA, such as C15:0, albeit in smaller quantities, suggests that the availability of FA in the diet of both groups varies significantly. (Bec et al. 2003, Dalsgaard et al. 2003, Jaschinski et al. 2008).
